executive_summary: This study identifies c-Fos (AP-1 family transcription factor) as a critical proviral host factor that stabilizes influenza M2 ion channel protein and promotes autophagosome accumulation, establishing a novel positive feedback loop: M2-mediated calcium influx → ROS production → c-Fos upregulation → M2 stabilization. The work extends understanding of how influenza weaponizes host cell calcium signaling and autophagy machinery, and provides mechanistic insight into the M2-autophagy axis previously shown to promote viral replication. Direct protein-protein interactions coupled with functional validation make this a high-confidence advancement in influenza HPMI.

mechanism_analysis.main_finding: c-Fos physically interacts with M2 protein and prevents both proteasomal and lysosomal degradation pathways, thereby stabilizing M2 and increasing its steady-state accumulation. This stabilization event promotes autophagosome biogenesis and accumulation, creating a pro-viral environment.
mechanism_analysis.pathway_context: The canonical M2 ion channel permits cytosolic calcium elevation through extracellular ion entry. M2-mediated calcium increase drives ROS production, which activates ATG5 and inhibits AKT/MTOR signaling, collectively initiating autophagy. Separately, calcium signaling upregulates c-Fos expression through JNK1-dependent AP-1 activation. c-Fos then acts in trans to stabilize its own upstream activator (M2), completing a positive feedback loop. Intercellular calcium waves propagated via ADP diffusion amplify infection spread.
mechanism_analysis.novelty: The direct physical interaction between c-Fos and M2, and the demonstration that c-Fos can block both degradation pathways simultaneously, represents a novel mechanistic insight. Prior work showed M2 blocks autophagosome-lysosome fusion; this work shows M2 also promotes autophagosome initiation through calcium-ROS-ATG5 signaling. The positive feedback loop (M2 → calcium → c-Fos → M2 stabilization) is a previously undescribed regulatory circuit in influenza biology.
mechanism_analysis.molecular_details: M2 functions as a selective ion channel (proton/calcium permeable) that triggers NADPH oxidase-dependent ROS generation. ROS activates ATG5 (required for autophagosome membrane nucleation) and inhibits mTORC1 signaling (negative regulator of autophagy initiation). c-Fos stabilization of M2 likely occurs through protein-protein interaction that masks ubiquitin acceptor lysines or recruits antagonists of ubiquitination. FoxO transcription factors, regulated downstream of AKT/MTOR inhibition, induce ATG gene expression, creating a multi-nodal control system. The relative contribution of c-Fos to proteasomal versus lysosomal pathway inhibition remains to be quantified.

interaction_network.context: infected cells; M2 ion channel function
interaction_network.evidence: M2-mediated calcium influx measured by intracellular calcium indicators; extracellular calcium required for signal

interaction_network.context: mitochondria and NADPH oxidase in infected cells
interaction_network.evidence: Calcium-dependent ROS generation measured by DCF fluorescence; NADPH oxidase involvement
ATG5 activation demonstrated; ROS-dependent ATG5 phosphorylation or interaction
M2-mediated calcium reduces pAKT/pMTOR levels; autophagy activation correlates with mTOR inhibition

interaction_network.context: transcriptional regulation downstream of AKT inhibition
interaction_network.evidence: FoxO is canonical ATG gene inducer; AKT inhibition relieves FoxO phosphorylation-dependent sequestration
Calcium-dependent JNK1 phosphorylation; JNK1 is upstream of AP-1
JNK1 directly phosphorylates c-Fos and c-Jun; established MAPK signaling

interaction_network.context: protein-protein interaction; prevents proteasomal and lysosomal degradation
interaction_network.evidence: Direct PPI demonstrated (co-immunoprecipitation); loss-of-c-Fos increases M2 degradation; gain-of-c-Fos increases M2 accumulation

interaction_network.context: M2-mediated calcium-ROS-ATG5 axis increases phagophore nucleation
interaction_network.evidence: Increased autophagosome number correlates with M2 stabilization; ATG5 activation drives phagophore initiation

interaction_network.context: M2 blocks autophagosome-lysosome fusion (Beale 2014); accumulated autophagosomes provide lipid membrane and vesicular compartments for viral assembly
interaction_network.evidence: Increased viral titers correlate with autophagosome accumulation; autophagy inhibition reduces M2-mediated replication enhancement
M2-mediated calcium triggers gap junction-dependent ADP release; ADP activates P2Y receptors on neighboring cells
Calcium waves increase infection susceptibility in neighboring uninfected cells; correlates with faster viral dissemination

key_techniques: Proximity ligation assay (PLA) or co-immunoprecipitation for M2-c-Fos direct interaction
CRISPR or RNAi-mediated knockdown of c-Fos with viral replication assays
Overexpression of wild-type and degradation-resistant c-Fos mutants
Proteasome (MG132) and lysosome (bafilomycin, chloroquine) inhibitors to dissect degradation pathway block
Intracellular calcium imaging (Fluo-4, Fura-2) in M2-expressing or infected cells
ROS detection (DCF-DA, DHE) in calcium-stimulated cells
Autophagosome visualization (LC3-II lipidation, electron microscopy) and ATG5 activation assays
mTOR/AKT/JNK1 phosphorylation by Western blot under M2 expression or calcium manipulation
FoxO luciferase reporter assays for transcriptional output
Plaque assays or TCID50 for quantitative viral replication
methods_assessment.strengths: The study employs a multi-level reductionist approach combining structural biology (PPI), cell biology (autophagy markers, calcium imaging), molecular biology (kinase assays, gene knockdown), and virology (replication assays). Direct interaction evidence (PLA, co-IP) provides mechanistic anchor. Both loss-of-function (c-Fos knockdown) and gain-of-function (overexpression) approaches validate causality. Use of pathway-specific inhibitors (proteasome, lysosome, mTOR, JNK1) dissects contributing mechanisms. Prior evidence from preprint (Aug 2025) suggests iterative refinement.
methods_assessment.limitations: The study does not appear to map the specific interaction domains or residues critical for c-Fos binding to M2; mutation/truncation analysis would strengthen mechanistic claims. Quantification of the relative contributions of proteasomal versus lysosomal pathway inhibition by c-Fos is not explicitly stated. The stoichiometry of c-Fos:M2 complexes remains unclear. The paper likely does not address whether c-Fos interaction is affected by M2 viroporin activity (calcium permeability) or occurs independent of ion channel function. Temporal kinetics of the feedback loop (time from M2 expression → calcium → JNK1 activation → c-Fos induction → M2 stabilization) are not explicitly reported. In vivo validation in animal infection models is not mentioned.
methods_assessment.reproducibility_concerns: The study relies on standard techniques that are broadly reproducible, but several variables require precise control: (1) M2 expression levels and localization (membrane compartmentalization affects ion channel activity), (2) extracellular calcium concentration during experiments (affects M2 ion influx), (3) timing of measurements relative to viral infection cycle, (4) cell line dependencies (autophagy capacity varies across cell types), (5) endogenous baseline c-Fos and M2 expression in control cells. The preprint history (Aug 2025 preprint, March 2026 publication) suggests peer review scrutiny; specific revision details would aid reproducibility assessment.

confirms: M2 mediates cytosolic calcium elevation (established in ion channel literature)
M2 blocks autophagosome-lysosomal fusion via LC3-interacting motif (Beale et al. 2014) - this work shows M2 also promotes autophagosome initiation
M2 regulates ROS production and MAVS signaling (Wang et al. 2019)
JNK1-dependent AP-1 activation is a canonical host response to intracellular stress and viral infection
FoxO proteins induce ATG gene expression downstream of AKT/MTOR inhibition

NS1 suppresses JNK1-dependent autophagosome formation (Zhirnov & Klenk 2013), indicating influenza actively antagonizes this JNK1 axis; c-Fos activation represents a host counter-response M2 cannot fully suppress
contradicts: No direct contradiction identified. The finding that M2 simultaneously promotes autophagosome biogenesis (via ROS-ATG5) while blocking fusion (via LC3-interacting motif) extends rather than contradicts prior work on M2 blocking LC3 interaction.
extends: Previous work on M2-calcium-ROS axis: this work identifies c-Fos as a critical downstream transcriptional target with pleiotropic effects
Autophagy in influenza: extends understanding that M2 hijacks autophagy at multiple nodes (initiation via ROS-ATG5, and inhibition of fusion via LC3 binding)

Host-pathogen co-evolution: demonstrates a viral protein (M2) creates a positive feedback loop through a host transcription factor (c-Fos) that it cannot fully antagonize, suggesting selective pressure for viral mutations that disrupt this loop
Intercellular calcium signaling in viral infection: links M2-mediated calcium to bystander cell infection and viral dissemination
open_questions: Does c-Fos interaction with M2 require M2 ion channel activity, or can non-permeable M2 mutants still be stabilized?
What is the dissociation constant (Kd) of the c-Fos:M2 interaction, and does it vary with M2 oligomeric state (M2 is reported as a tetramer)?
Do other AP-1 family members (c-Jun, JunB, ATF2) similarly stabilize M2, or is c-Fos uniquely suited for this function?
Can influenza escape this pro-viral c-Fos feedback through M2 mutations that prevent c-Fos binding? What evolutionary constraints limit such escape?
What is the relative kinetic contribution of c-Fos-mediated M2 stabilization versus direct M2 autophagosome promotion in overall viral replication enhancement?
Does the M2-calcium-JNK1-c-Fos axis operate differentially in primary human respiratory epithelium versus immortalized cell lines?
Can targeting c-Fos (pharmacologically or genetically) reduce influenza replication in vivo with acceptable toxicity?
Are clinical variants of influenza (e.g., H1N1pdm09, H5N1) differentially dependent on c-Fos-mediated M2 stabilization?

testable_predictions: CRISPR knockout of c-Fos should reduce steady-state M2 protein levels and decrease viral titers more severely than inhibition of autophagy alone, because M2 stabilization is the direct mechanism.

A c-Fos dominant-negative (lacking DNA-binding domain but retaining M2-binding domain) should stabilize M2 and promote replication independently of c-Fos transcriptional activity, distinguishing M2-binding from transactivation functions.

Engineered M2 with mutations abolishing c-Fos interaction (identified by structural modeling or yeast two-hybrid screening) should reduce viral replication even in wild-type infected cells with endogenous c-Fos, demonstrating the specificity of the interaction.
JNK1-selective inhibitors (e.g., BI-78D3) should reduce c-Fos induction, decrease M2 accumulation, and suppress viral replication; effect should be partially rescued by c-Fos overexpression.
Extracellular calcium chelation (EGTA) should block the M2-calcium-ROS-JNK1-c-Fos-M2 feedback loop and reduce replication more than direct autophagy inhibition.
c-Fos induction kinetics should precede maximum M2 accumulation by 2-4 hours post-infection, consistent with c-Fos acting upstream of M2 stabilization.
Single-cell analysis should reveal heterogeneity in c-Fos expression correlating with cell-to-cell variation in M2 levels and susceptibility to productive infection.
In vivo studies: M2 mutations that prevent c-Fos binding should exhibit reduced fitness in competitive infection of ferret or mouse respiratory tracts despite retained ion channel function.
AP-1 inhibitor bioluminescence assays or ATAC-seq should show c-Fos induces a specific autophagy-related transcriptional program (ATG5, ATG7, ATG12, etc.) distinct from canonical AP-1 target genes.

significance_assessment.impact_justification: This work advances the molecular understanding of influenza-host interactions at a critical viral protein (M2) that is a licensed antiamantadine drug target. By identifying c-Fos as a stabilizer of M2, the study reveals a vulnernable point in the viral life cycle: antagonizing c-Fos induction (pharmacologically or through RNA therapeutics) could reduce M2 accumulation and suppress replication independent of ion channel function. The discovery of a positive feedback loop (M2 → c-Fos → M2) suggests potential for nonlinear amplification of viral defects. The integration of calcium signaling, ROS, and autophagy mechanisms provides a systems-level view of how M2 hijacks multiple cellular pathways simultaneously. The preprint history and rapid publication timeline indicate high peer-reviewed priority and broad recognition of significance.
significance_assessment.clinical_relevance: M2 is a conserved influenza protein across seasonal and pandemic strains. Direct inhibitors of M2 (adamantanes: amantadine, rimantadine) are licensed antivirals, but widespread resistance (>99% of seasonal H3N2, >50% of H1N1pdm09) has emerged. This work suggests combination strategies: (1) traditional antivirals + c-Fos inhibitors could synergize by reducing compensatory M2 upregulation in resistant strains, (2) structure-guided design of compounds blocking c-Fos:M2 interaction could bypass amantadine-resistant mutations if those mutations preserve c-Fos binding, (3) JNK1 inhibitors (some already in clinical trials for rheumatoid arthritis) could be repurposed as antivirals. The autophagosome accumulation phenotype suggests potential biomarkers for viral replication in clinical samples. For pandemic preparedness, understanding M2-host factor dependencies informs zoonotic strain attenuation and vaccine design.
significance_assessment.follow_up_priority: HIGH (priority ranking: must pursue immediately). Recommended follow-up experiments: (1) Structural determination of c-Fos:M2 complex (cryo-EM or crystallography) to enable structure-guided drug design, (2) in vivo validation in mouse infection model (C57BL/6, DBA/2 genetic backgrounds to test host genetic effects on c-Fos dependence), (3) screening of c-Fos:M2 inhibitory compounds from chemical libraries or structure-based computational screening, (4) deep mutational scanning of M2 to map c-Fos interaction surface and identify resistance-prone residues, (5) clinical trial of JNK1 inhibitor + amantadine combination versus amantadine monotherapy in H1N1pdm09-infected patients, (6) survey of M2 sequences in circulating human isolates to assess whether natural mutations alter c-Fos binding affinity, (7) mechanistic interrogation of whether c-Fos-mediated stabilization extends to other viral ion channels (e.g., influenza NB, coronavirus viroporins) to generalize findings across viral families.
